question_title: Will the U.S. Copyright Office publish a report document labeled Part 4 in its Copyright and Artificial Intelligence series before May 1, 2026?
summary: The U.S. Copyright Office's AI report series was explicitly designed as a 3-part series. Multiple authoritative sources (Skadden, Wiley Law, McDermott Will & Emery, and the Copyright Office's own announcements) describe Part 3 as the "third and final" installment. As of March 29, 2026, no Part 4 exists on copyright.gov/ai/, no Part 4 is mentioned in any news or legal commentary, and the final version of Part 3 itself still hasn't been published (only a pre-publication version from May 2025 exists). The Copyright Office faces ongoing institutional turmoil with the Register's job status being litigated before the Supreme Court. All topics from the original 2023 Notice of Inquiry have been addressed across Parts 1-3. With only 31 days until the May 1, 2026 deadline, publication of a Part 4 is near-impossible.

factors.description: Series explicitly designed as 3 parts: multiple authoritative legal sources describe Part 3 as 'third and final' installment. The Part 2 announcement states 'report is being released in three Parts'.
No Part 4 exists on copyright.gov/ai/ page as of March 29, 2026. No announcements, no mention of Part 4 anywhere in news, legal commentary, or official sources.
Part 3 final version still not published (only pre-publication version from May 2025). If the Office hasn't finalized Part 3, publishing Part 4 in 31 days is implausible.
Leadership turmoil: Register Perlmutter fired May 2025, reinstated by courts, Supreme Court case pending. Institutional instability reduces capacity for new major publications.
All topics from the original August 2023 NOI covered across Parts 1-3 (digital replicas, copyrightability, training, licensing, liability). No unaddressed topic to motivate Part 4.
Only 31 days remain until May 1, 2026 deadline. Major policy reports require months of internal review and drafting.

reasoning:
**Forecast: 3% probability (NO)**

The evidence overwhelmingly points to NO resolution:

1. **Series is complete at 3 parts.** The Copyright Office's own announcements and multiple major law firms (Skadden, Wiley, McDermott) describe Part 3 as the "third and final" installment. The series was explicitly scoped to three parts.

2. **No Part 4 exists or is announced.** The copyright.gov/ai/ page lists only Parts 1-3. No news, legal commentary, or official communication mentions a Part 4.

3. **Part 3 final version still unpublished.** Only a "pre-publication version" from May 2025 exists. The Office hasn't even completed its existing series.

4. **Institutional turmoil.** Register Perlmutter was fired the day after Part 3's pre-publication, reinstated by courts, and her status remains before the Supreme Court. This severely limits the Office's capacity for major new publications.

5. **All NOI topics addressed.** Digital replicas, copyrightability, training, licensing, and liability — all covered across Parts 1-3.

6. **Only 31 days remain.** Major policy reports take months to prepare; there's no possibility of producing one from scratch in this window.
sources_consulted: [Taking a Closer Look: What the Copyright Office’s 2025 AI Reports Mean for 2026](https://www.nealandleroy.com/post/taking-a-closer-look-what-the-copyright-office-s-2025-ai-reports-mean-for-2026)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves to yes if the U.S. Copyright Office publishes a report document labeled Part 4 in its Copyright and Artificial Intelligence series before May 1, 2026. It resolves to no otherwise. The primary source is the Copyright Office AI page at https://www.copyright.gov/ai/
fine_print: The document must be publicly posted by the U.S. Copyright Office and clearly identified as Part 4 of the series. A speech, blog post, or testimony mentioning Part 4 is not sufficient. Candidate Sources: Copyright Office policy reports page https://www.copyright.gov/policy/
